evidence: Argyle et al. introduce silicon sampling and demonstrate its viability via demographic conditioning of GPT-3
Paper defines algorithmic fidelity and provides empirical validation on ANES and Pigeonholing Partisans
Provides empirical evidence that GPT-3 with demographic backstories replicates ANES subpopulation response distributions
Shows that intersectional multi-attribute demographic conditioning outperforms single-attribute conditioning for attitude replication
Algorithmic fidelity is the key evaluation property for assessing silicon sampling quality
Introduced the memory stream: a persistent natural-language record of agent experiences, retrieved by recency+importance+relevance scoring.
Introduced agent reflection: periodic synthesis of raw observations into higher-level inferences stored back into the memory stream.
Introduced the full generative agent architecture (memory stream + reflection + planning) instantiated in the Smallville sandbox.
Human evaluators rated full-architecture agents more believable than ablations and crowd-workers; emergent social dynamics observed over 2 simulated days in Smallville.
Ablation study with 100 human evaluators: each component (memory, reflection, planning) independently degrades believability when removed.
Reflection objects are stored in the memory stream and retrieved alongside raw observations.
Reflection is a core component of the generative agent architecture alongside memory and planning.
Memory stream is the persistence layer of the generative agent architecture.
Five canonical behavioral economics experiments reproduced qualitatively with frontier LLMs
Calibrated mixture of theory-grounded personas halves out-of-sample MSE vs persona-less agents on Charness-Rabin two-stage games
Introduces Homo silicus as a conceptual framework for using LLMs as implicit computational models of humans
Demonstrates theory-grounded persona conditioning and calibrated agent mixtures for improving simulation fidelity
Concordia library introduces and implements GABM as a distinct modeling paradigm for LLM-powered social simulation
Concordia demonstrates GABM across six application domains with illustrative qualitative evidence
GABM is a core technical approach within the multi-agent social simulation research direction
Concordia directly implements LLM-powered human simulacra for social science research
Introduces OpinionQA dataset and framework for evaluating LM opinion alignment with human demographic groups via Pew ATP surveys
OpinionQA evaluation of 9 LMs shows substantial misalignment with US demographic groups; RLHF amplifies skew toward liberal/educated/high-income groups

Systematic review of 63 papers (2023-2025) finds 43% target 'general population,' 35% discuss representativeness, 65% omit sociodemographic details — establishing widespread persona underspecification
Introduces and empirically motivates the Persona Transparency Checklist through review of 63 NLP/AI persona studies
Core position: five challenges (diversity, bias, sycophancy, alienness, generalization) are tractable with identified promising directions
Reviews evidence from Hewitt et al. (91% treatment effect variation), Park et al. (85% retest), Binz et al. (Centaur) supporting simulation accuracy
Acknowledges diversity and bias as key challenges; cites narrower political opinion distributions and overrepresentation of WEIRD demographics
Comprehensive review of LLM social simulation methods, identifying five tractable challenges and promising directions for silicon sampling
Proposes interview-based prompting, LLM-as-expert prompting, and distribution elicitation as advanced persona conditioning variants
Decomposes fidelity into five dimensions: diversity, bias, sycophancy, alienness, generalization; proposes iterative evaluation framework
Builds on silicon sampling framework; reviews and contextualizes Argyle et al.'s foundational results within broader five-challenge agenda
Contextualizes generative agents as an example of LLM social simulation; discusses interview-based prompting building on Park et al.'s 1052-agent approach
Builds on Santurkar et al.'s findings about opinion distribution skew as evidence for diversity and bias challenges
